attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-week history of peeling of his feet. He reports scaling and cracking between his toes, which becomes worse after swimming, and is mildly pruritic. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He works as a lifeguard at a local swimming pool. He notices recurrent viral infections during the winter and has a 4-year old child at daycare.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is moist, macerated skin with scaling in the fourth and fifth toe web spaces bilaterally. The surrounding skin shows erythema with a peripheral scale and central clearing extending onto the lateral aspects of the feet. There is no bleeding or purulent discharge.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of tinea pedis (athlete's foot), including pruritus, interdigital maceration, and peripheral scaling with central clearing. Patient may also present with erythema and hyperkeratotic plaques with scales which is mildly or not pruiritic, or painful vesicles on an erythematous background. The occupational exposure as a lifeguard increases his risk due to frequent exposure to warm, moist environments. It is a very common superficial fungal infection with global prevalence about 3%.

Potassium hydroxide microscopy is the most appropriate initial investigation as it provides rapid confirmation of fungal elements. Skin scrapings are examined under the microscope after treatment with potassium hydroxide, which dissolves keratin and allows visualization of fungal hyphae. This test is quick, inexpensive, and highly specific when positive.

Bacterial culture and sensitivity would be appropriate if bacterial infection was suspected, such as in cases with purulent discharge or cellulitis. While secondary bacterial infection can occur with tinea pedis, the presentation here is typical for fungal infection. Furthermore, samples from the macerated skin with tinea pedis may often culture concomitant staphylococcus aureus or psuedomonas aeruginosa leading to incorrect diagnosis. Skin scrapings should be taken instead from the edge of the lesions.

Skin biopsy is overly invasive for diagnosing tinea pedis. It would be considered if there was diagnostic uncertainty or concern for other conditions such as psoriasis or eczema that had failed to respond to conventional therapy.

Patch testing is used to diagnose allergic contact dermatitis. While contact dermatitis can affect the feet, the pattern of involvement and the presence of peripheral scaling with central clearing is more consistent with tinea.

Blood glucose level testing might be considered in cases of recurrent or resistant fungal infections as diabetes is a risk factor. In this patient's case, the history of recurrent viral infections is common with parents of children who attend childcare. However, it is not the most appropriate initial investigation for confirming the diagnosis of tinea pedis in an otherwise healthy young adult.
learningPoints: Tinea pedis classically presents with pruritic, scaly erythema in the web spaces between toes.
The presence of peripheral scaling with central clearing is characteristic of dermatophyte infections.
Potassium hydroxide microscopy is the most rapid diagnostic test for confirming fungal infection.
references: Kovitwanichkanont T, Chong A. Superficial Fungal Infections. Australian Journal of General Practice. 2019: 48 (10). Available at: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2019/october/superficial-fungal-infections
Leung AK, Barankin B, Lam JM, Leong KF, Hon KL. Tinea pedis: an updated review. Drugs Context. 2023 Jun 29;12:2023-5-1.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10321471/
